title: A Further Look at the Oscillator Semigroup and the eta Correspondence for Dual Pairs Over Finite Fields
speaker: Roger Howe
tags: Special Joint IAS/PU Group Theory Afternoon

proof_stages.label: Rank as a size invariant for classical-group representations
proof_stages.summary: The talk replaces dimension by a rank invariant obtained by restricting a representation to abelian unipotent radicals of parabolics and recording the largest rank of the characters or orbits that occur.

proof_stages.label: Heisenberg and oscillator representations as minimal-rank sources
proof_stages.summary: Using the Stone-von Neumann theorem for the Heisenberg group, the symplectic action gives the Weil/oscillator representation, which over finite fields is an honest small representation and whose tensor powers build higher ranks.

proof_stages.label: Dual pairs and the restriction problem
proof_stages.summary: A dual pair is introduced as a pair of mutual centralizers, giving a natural framework for restricting the oscillator representation; the familiar local-field theta correspondence is contrasted with its failure to be bijective over finite fields.

proof_stages.label: Classical dual-pair series and rank control
proof_stages.summary: Classical dual pairs such as orthogonal-symplectic and general-linear pairs occur in compatible series, and the embeddings/tensor behavior of their oscillator representations let one vary a partner to isolate representations of prescribed rank.

proof_stages.label: Rank-k finite-field eta/theta correspondence
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem says that, for k<n, restricting the oscillator representation for an orthogonal-symplectic pair identifies symplectic representations with unipotent spectrum determined by a rank-k quadratic form and injects the dual of the orthogonal group into the rank-k symplectic dual, in fact exhausting the rank-k representations.

proof_stages.label: Range of the theorem and exceptional-group limits
proof_stages.summary: Questions clarify the condition k<n and emphasize that the construction is presently tied to systematic classical series, with no comparable rank theory known for exceptional groups.

proof_stages.label: Oscillator semigroup completion
proof_stages.summary: The symplectic group is enlarged from graphs of symplectic isomorphisms to all Lagrangian correspondences in W⊕W⁻, producing the oscillator semigroup, which restores mutual-commutant generation for dual pairs over finite fields and raises the problem of its representations.

examples.name: GL_n parabolic with a matrix unipotent radical
examples.note: The block parabolic in GL_n gives a concrete case where characters of an abelian unipotent radical are organized by ordinary matrix rank, illustrating the representation-rank definition.
examples.engagement_reason: he uses the GL_n parabolic concretely, identifies its unipotent radical with a matrix space, analyzes ranks, character orbits, and how a GL_n representation restricts to it

examples.name: Complex versus real symmetric matrices
examples.note: The comparison that over C symmetric matrices are classified by rank while over R there are several signature types illustrates why the relevant spectrum may refine numerical rank.
examples.engagement_reason: he only states the contrast that complex symmetric matrices have one orbit per rank while real ones have several, without deriving or using it further

passes.reason: Real symmetric matrices of the same rank but different signatures show that the complex one-orbit-per-rank behavior fails over R.

examples.name: The one-dimensional finite-field pair inside SL_2
examples.note: The simplest finite-field dual-pair restriction gives every GL_1 character once but the trivial character twice, visibly showing why naive theta bijectivity fails and motivating a rank-refined replacement.
examples.engagement_reason: he analyzes the GO_1 x GO_1 pair inside SL_2 over a finite field and computes the resulting character decomposition, including the extra trivial character

passes.reason: The GO(1) x GO(1) case inside SL_2 is the minimal dual-pair case and is used to show finite-field theta restriction is not bijective or multiplicity-free.
the minimal one-dimensional dual-pair case in SL_2 is explicitly used to show finite-field theta restriction is not bijective

the smallest one-dimensional dual-pair case is used to show the finite-field correspondence is not bijective
